food_name: Ayam Masak Merah (chicken in spiced tomato gravy)
region_of_origin: Malay Peninsula — Singapore and Malaysia Malay home and hawker cooking

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Ayam Masak Merah. Closest parent is chicken in spiced tomato-based curry sauce.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken in spiced tomato-coconut gravy (Malay masak merah style)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Ayam Masak Merah serving is one piece of bone-in, skin-on chicken (approximately 150g) with 100g of the spiced tomato-coconut gravy. Served alongside steamed rice (not included in these values).

nutrient_source.title: Ayam Masak Merah — HPB Singapore Foodo; cross-referenced with IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore; Institute for Medical Research (IMR) Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD both have entries for Ayam Masak Merah. Values per 100g represent the chicken and gravy combined at approximately 60%:40% ratio. Chicken is bone-in, skin-on — edible yield is approximately 65% of bone-in weight.

gi_source: GI not applicable to the curry component alone. The small amount of carbohydrate (5.2g/100g) from tomatoes and minor thickening agents does not produce a meaningful glycaemic response. The composite GI with steamed rice is approximately 65.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium from spice paste preparation (salt, possible fish sauce or dried shrimp in paste), tomato (naturally low sodium, canned tomato adds variable sodium). A 250g serving delivers approximately 963mg sodium. Moderate for a Malay curry.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should consume ayam masak merah in moderation — once or twice per week. The tomato base is lower in sodium than dried shrimp or belacan-heavy curries. Remove chicken skin before eating to reduce fat and allow better meal frequency.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: The curry component has low carbohydrate (5.2g/100g) — glycaemic impact comes from the accompanying steamed rice. Tomato lycopene and the moderate fat content from coconut milk may reduce insulin resistance over time with regular consumption.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes can include ayam masak merah as a main protein-rich side dish with controlled rice portion. The chicken skin adds saturated fat without carbohydrate. Remove skin to reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Tomato lycopene provides anti-oxidative benefit. Coconut milk saturated fat (4.8g/100g) is a concern for systemic inflammation. The spice base (galangal, star anise, ginger, dried chilli) has anti-inflammatory properties.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ayam masak merah is acceptable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis, particularly for its tomato lycopene content. Remove chicken skin to reduce saturated fat. Limit to 2 times per week due to the coconut milk saturated fat component.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Masak Merah, literally 'red-cooked chicken', is a festive Malay chicken dish characterised by its vibrant red-orange tomato-chilli gravy. Unlike Indian chicken curry (coconut milk and yogurt base) or Chinese braised chicken (soy-based), Ayam Masak Merah uses canned or fresh tomatoes as the primary sauce base, combined with dried chilli, onion, garlic, and a small amount of coconut milk for richness. The chicken is typically pan-fried first to caramelise the skin, then simmered in the tomato gravy until the sauce reduces and coats the chicken. It is a staple Hari Raya dish and common at Malay hawker rice stalls. The tomato base provides lycopene, and the spice blend offers anti-inflammatory compounds.

confidence_reason: Direct entries in HPB Singapore Foodo and IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Consistent values across both sources. High confidence in macro and micronutrient values.
notes: Values are for bone-in, skin-on chicken with gravy — edible yield (chicken without bone) is approximately 65% of the reported serving weight. Removing chicken skin reduces fat by approximately 3–5g per 100g serving. Tomato provides lycopene (4–8mg per serving), which has cardiovascular and anti-cancer benefits. The tomato-coconut combination is lower in saturated fat than full rendang but higher than asam pedas.
